title: Connection Error
why: The server didn't respond at all — DNS failure, timeout, or a refused connection. Search engines that can't reach a URL can't index it, and users hit a dead end.
howToFix: Confirm the URL is correct and the host is reachable. Check DNS, server uptime, firewall rules, and that your server isn't rate-limiting the crawler.
impact: Completely invisible to search engines and inaccessible to users.

title: Server Error (5xx)
why: A 5xx means your server failed to deliver the page. Persistent 5xx errors cause search engines to slow crawling and eventually drop the URL from the index.
howToFix: Inspect server logs for the failing request, fix the underlying application/server fault, and return a 200 once healthy. Use 503 with Retry-After only for planned maintenance.
impact: Lost rankings and crawl budget; users can't access the page.

title: Client Error (4xx)
why: A 4xx (usually 404) means the page is gone or the URL is wrong. Internal links pointing to 4xx pages waste crawl budget and leak link equity into dead ends.
howToFix: Restore the page, or 301-redirect the URL to the best alternative, and fix any internal links that point to it. Return a proper 404 for genuinely removed content.
impact: Broken user journeys and wasted crawl budget; equity from inbound links is lost.

title: Redirect (3xx)
why: A 3xx adds a round-trip before the real content loads and dilutes link signals slightly. A few are fine; many internal links to redirects add up.
howToFix: Update internal links to point straight at the final destination URL so crawlers and users skip the hop.
impact: Slower loads and minor signal dilution across the site.

title: Redirect Chain
why: Multiple hops (A→B→C) compound latency and risk a hop breaking. Some crawlers stop following after a few redirects, abandoning the final page.
howToFix: Collapse the chain so the first URL redirects directly to the final destination in a single hop.
impact: Wasted crawl budget, slower pages, and a risk the destination is never reached.

title: Slow Response
why: Slow server responses (high TTFB) hurt Core Web Vitals, frustrate users, and limit how many pages a crawler will fetch per visit.
howToFix: Add caching/CDN, optimize database queries, enable keep-alive and compression, and move to faster hosting if needed.
impact: Lower rankings via Core Web Vitals and reduced crawl coverage.

title: Broken Link
why: A link on this page points to a URL that returns an error. Broken links erode trust, waste crawl budget, and send users and bots to dead ends.
howToFix: Fix the link target, update it to the correct URL, or remove the link. Re-check periodically as external targets change.
impact: Poor user experience and leaked link equity into broken destinations.

title: Orphan Page
why: No internal links point to this page, so users and crawlers can only reach it via the sitemap or external links. Orphans get crawled rarely and rank poorly.
howToFix: Add internal links to the page from relevant, in-context locations (navigation, related content, hub pages).
impact: Reduced discoverability, crawl frequency, and ranking potential.

title: Deep Page
why: This page is many clicks from the homepage. Pages buried deep in the architecture receive less crawl attention and less internal link equity.
howToFix: Flatten your site architecture so important pages sit within ~3 clicks of the homepage via hub pages and contextual links.
impact: Slower indexing and weaker rankings for deep content.

title: Missing Title
why: The title tag is the single most important on-page SEO element and the clickable headline in search results. Without one, engines invent one — usually badly.
howToFix: Add a unique, descriptive <title> of roughly 30–60 characters that includes the page's primary keyword near the front.
impact: Drastically reduced click-through and ranking for the page.

title: Title Too Long
why: Titles over ~60 characters get truncated in search results, hiding the end of your message and reducing click-through.
howToFix: Tighten the title to under ~60 characters, front-loading the most important words.
impact: Truncated, less compelling search snippets.

title: Title Too Short
why: Very short titles waste valuable space in the search result and often omit useful keywords searchers use.
howToFix: Expand the title to ~30–60 characters with descriptive, keyword-relevant wording.
impact: Missed keyword relevance and weaker click-through.

title: Duplicate Title
why: Multiple pages share the same title, so search engines struggle to tell them apart and may pick the wrong one to rank.
howToFix: Give every page a unique title that reflects its specific content.
impact: Keyword cannibalisation and diluted ranking signals.

title: Missing Meta Description
why: The meta description is your ad copy in search results. Without one, engines auto-generate a snippet from page text, often unappealing.
howToFix: Write a unique, compelling 70–160 character description that summarises the page and invites the click.
impact: Lower click-through from search results.

title: Meta Description Too Long
why: Descriptions beyond ~160 characters are truncated, cutting off your call to action.
howToFix: Trim to ~70–160 characters and put the key message first.
impact: Truncated snippets with weaker messaging.

title: Meta Description Too Short
why: Very short descriptions under-use the snippet space and rarely persuade the searcher.
howToFix: Expand to ~70–160 characters with a clear summary and benefit.
impact: Under-leveraged search real estate.

title: Duplicate Meta Description
why: Reused descriptions across pages make snippets generic and signal thin differentiation to search engines.
howToFix: Write a distinct description for each page.
impact: Generic snippets and weaker differentiation.

title: Missing H1
why: The H1 is the page's main on-page heading; it tells users and engines (and increasingly AI answer engines) what the page is about.
howToFix: Add exactly one clear, descriptive <h1> that summarises the page's topic.
impact: Weaker topical clarity for search and AI extraction.

title: Multiple H1
why: Several H1s blur the page's primary topic and weaken the heading hierarchy that crawlers and screen readers rely on.
howToFix: Keep a single H1 and demote the rest to H2/H3 to form a clean outline.
impact: Diluted topical focus and accessibility issues.

title: Noindex
why: A noindex directive tells search engines to keep this page out of their index. Intentional for thank-you/admin pages — disastrous if applied by mistake.
howToFix: Confirm the noindex is intended. If the page should rank, remove the noindex from the meta robots tag or X-Robots-Tag header.
impact: The page cannot appear in search results.

title: Nofollow
why: A page-level nofollow stops link equity flowing from this page's links, which can strand the pages it links to.
howToFix: Remove the blanket nofollow unless you deliberately want to seal off link flow from this page.
impact: Reduced crawl discovery and equity distribution.

title: X-Robots-Tag: noindex
why: A noindex set via HTTP header is easy to overlook because it isn't visible in the HTML, yet it removes the page from search just the same.
howToFix: Audit your server/CDN config and remove the X-Robots-Tag noindex if the page should be indexable.
impact: Silent de-indexing that's hard to spot.

title: Blocked by robots.txt
why: robots.txt is preventing crawlers from fetching this URL. Blocked pages can't be crawled, and if linked, may show up in results with no snippet.
howToFix: If the page should be crawled, remove or narrow the Disallow rule. Use noindex (not robots.txt) to keep a page out of the index.
impact: The page's content is invisible to search engines.

title: Missing Canonical
why: Without a canonical tag, search engines guess which URL version is authoritative, risking duplicate-content splits across parameters and variants.
howToFix: Add a self-referencing <link rel="canonical"> on each page pointing to its preferred URL.
impact: Duplicate-content ambiguity and split ranking signals.

title: Canonicalised
why: This page's canonical points to a different URL, so engines treat the other URL as the one to index. Fine when deliberate, harmful when accidental.
howToFix: Verify the canonical target is correct. If this page should rank on its own, point the canonical at itself.
impact: The page may be dropped from the index in favour of the canonical target.

title: Images Missing Alt Text
why: Alt text describes images to screen-reader users and to search engines (powering image search). Missing alt hurts accessibility and discoverability.
howToFix: Add concise, descriptive alt attributes to meaningful images; use empty alt="" for purely decorative ones.
impact: Accessibility failures and lost image-search traffic.

title: Thin Content
why: Pages with very little content rarely satisfy search intent and are easily out-competed. AI answer engines also skip pages that lack substance.
howToFix: Expand the page with genuinely useful, original content that fully answers the user's question.
impact: Weak rankings and low citation in AI answers.

title: Large Page Size
why: A heavy HTML payload slows rendering and parsing, hurting Core Web Vitals and mobile users on slow connections.
howToFix: Reduce inline scripts/styles, lazy-load below-the-fold content, and trim unnecessary markup.
impact: Slower loads and weaker Core Web Vitals scores.

title: Duplicate Content
why: Two or more pages share near-identical content. Search engines must choose one to rank and may pick the wrong one, splitting signals across the duplicates.
howToFix: Consolidate duplicates with 301 redirects or canonical tags, or differentiate the content so each page serves a distinct intent.
impact: Diluted rankings and wasted crawl budget.

title: Low Text-to-HTML Ratio
why: A page that is mostly markup with little readable text often signals thin or template-heavy content to search and AI engines.
howToFix: Increase the proportion of meaningful body text relative to code, and remove bloated markup.
impact: Perceived as low-value, reducing ranking and citation.

title: Not Served Over HTTPS
why: HTTPS is a confirmed ranking signal and a baseline trust requirement. Browsers flag HTTP pages as 'Not secure', scaring users away.
howToFix: Install a TLS certificate and 301-redirect all HTTP URLs to their HTTPS equivalents site-wide.
impact: Lower trust, browser warnings, and a ranking disadvantage.

title: Mixed Content
why: An HTTPS page is loading resources over insecure HTTP. Browsers block or warn on mixed content, breaking layout and eroding the security guarantee.
howToFix: Update all resource URLs (images, scripts, styles) to HTTPS, or use protocol-relative/upgrade-insecure-requests.
impact: Broken assets and browser security warnings.

title: No HSTS Header
why: Without HTTP Strict Transport Security, the first request can be downgraded to HTTP, exposing users to interception on that initial hop.
howToFix: Send a Strict-Transport-Security header with a long max-age once you're confident all subdomains are HTTPS.
impact: A small but real man-in-the-middle exposure window.

title: No Text Compression
why: Serving HTML without gzip/brotli sends far more bytes than necessary, slowing every page load.
howToFix: Enable gzip or brotli compression for text responses at your server or CDN.
impact: Slower page loads and higher bandwidth use.

title: Missing Viewport
why: The viewport meta tag is required for responsive layouts. Without it, mobile browsers render a zoomed-out desktop page that's unusable on phones.
howToFix: Add <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"> to the <head>.
impact: Broken mobile experience and mobile-first ranking penalties.

title: Missing Lang Attribute
why: The html lang attribute tells browsers, screen readers, and search engines what language the page is in.
howToFix: Set the language on the root element, e.g. <html lang="en">.
impact: Accessibility issues and weaker language targeting.

title: Incomplete hreflang
why: hreflang annotations help engines serve the right language/region version. Incomplete or non-reciprocal hreflang confuses targeting.
howToFix: Ensure every language variant lists all others (including itself) with valid language-region codes and a return link.
impact: Wrong-language pages shown to users in search results.

title: Missing Open Graph Tags
why: Open Graph tags control how the page looks when shared on social platforms and in chat apps. Without them, shares get an unattractive, generic preview.
howToFix: Add og:title, og:description, and og:image (plus a Twitter card) to the <head>.
impact: Poor social share previews and lower click-through from social.

title: Missing Twitter Card
why: Twitter/X card tags define the rich preview when your page is shared there.
howToFix: Add twitter:card (and optionally twitter:title/description/image) meta tags.
impact: Plain-text social previews that earn fewer clicks.

title: No Structured Data
why: Schema.org structured data unlocks rich results (stars, FAQs, breadcrumbs) and is increasingly how AI engines understand and cite a page.
howToFix: Add relevant JSON-LD (Article, Product, FAQPage, BreadcrumbList, Organization) matching the page's content.
impact: No rich results and reduced machine understanding of the page.

title: GEO: No Machine-Readable Structure
why: Generative engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) lean on structured data and clean semantics to understand and cite sources. Pages without it are harder to quote accurately.
howToFix: Add JSON-LD structured data and use semantic HTML so AI can parse entities, facts, and relationships reliably.
impact: Lower likelihood of being cited in AI-generated answers.

title: GEO: Not Answer-Ready
why: AI answer engines extract concise answers that sit directly under a clear heading. Pages that bury the answer in long prose are harder to quote.
howToFix: Lead each section with the direct answer in the first sentence after the heading, then elaborate. Use question-style headings.
impact: Passed over by AI engines in favour of more extractable competitors.

title: GEO: Missing Authorship / E-E-A-T
why: Generative and traditional engines weigh experience, expertise, authoritativeness, and trust. Clear authorship and dates are strong trust signals.
howToFix: Add a named author with credentials, a published/modified date, and author schema markup.
impact: Reduced trust weighting and citation likelihood.

title: GEO: Too Thin to Cite
why: AI engines cite substantive, self-contained passages. Very short pages rarely contain a citable, authoritative chunk.
howToFix: Add depth: definitions, specifics, data, and self-contained explanations an engine can lift verbatim.
impact: Little to quote means little chance of being referenced.

title: GEO: Weak Semantic Structure
why: Landmarks like <main>, <article>, and a clean heading outline help machines isolate the main content from navigation and boilerplate.
howToFix: Wrap primary content in <main>/<article>, and use a logical H1→H2→H3 outline.
impact: Harder for engines to separate content from chrome, lowering extraction quality.

title: GEO: AI-Ready Page
why: This page has the structure, semantics, and signals generative engines prefer — it's well positioned to be cited in AI answers.
howToFix: Maintain this: keep structured data current, answers near the top, and authorship clear.
impact: Strong candidate for inclusion in AI-generated answers.
